content:
# Numbers Chapter 23 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 176–177; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0130–0131 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_023_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE ORACLES THAT CANNOT BE REVERSED: BLESSING SPOKEN FROM THE HIGH PLACES:** Numbers 23 contains the first two of Balaam's four great oracles (the third and fourth follow in chapter 24). The chapter's architecture is tightly symmetrical: sacrificial preparation (vv. 1–6), first oracle (vv. 7–10), Balak's protest (vv. 11–12), relocation and second preparation (vv. 13–17), second oracle (vv. 18–24), and Balak's second protest (vv. 25–26), concluding with a third attempt at a new vantage point (vv. 27–30). The Hebrew verb שׁוּב (shuv, "to turn/reverse") becomes the chapter's theological keyword: what YHWH has blessed cannot be turned back. In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 23 begins on page 176 (continuing from chapter 22 on the same page) and extends through page 177. The text flows in the standard three-column layout with Masoretic marginal apparatus. In Codex Vaticanus, the chapter spans pa_0130–0131, with the section marker ΝΑ (= 51) visible at the opening of Balaam's first oracle, and ΝΒ (= 52) at the second. The LXX follows the MT closely through the narrative frames but shows interpretive variation in the poetic oracles, particularly in vv. 9–10 and 21–24.

---

## Sacrificial Preparation and the First Oracle (vv. 1–12)

**1** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] Balak [Balaq]: "Build [beneh] for-me [li] here [ba-zeh] seven [shiv'ah] altars [mizbekhot], and-prepare [ve-hakhen] for-me [li] here [ba-zeh] seven [shiv'ah] bulls [parim] and-seven [ve-shiv'ah] rams [eilim]."

> **Key terms:** שִׁבְעָה מִזְבְּחֹת (shiv'ah mizbekhot) = "seven altars" — the number seven is the number of completeness in ancient Near Eastern ritual, and Balaam's demand for seven altars, seven bulls, and seven rams constitutes an elaborate pagan-style divinatory rite. The verb הָכֵן (hakhen) = "to prepare/make ready" — from כון (kun), in the hiphil meaning to establish or set in readiness. The sacrificial animals — פָּרִים (parim, "bulls") and אֵילִם (eilim, "rams") — are the premier offerings of the ancient world. **LXX:** οἰκοδόμησόν μοι ἐνταῦθα ἑπτὰ βωμοὺς καὶ ἑτοίμασόν μοι ἐνταῦθα ἑπτὰ μόσχους καὶ ἑπτὰ κριούς ("build for me here seven altars and prepare for me here seven calves and seven rams") — rendering פָּרִים as μόσχους ("calves") rather than "bulls," continuing the LXX's pattern noted at 22:4.

**2** And-did [va-ya'as] Balak [Balaq] as-which [ka-asher] spoke [dibber] Balaam [Bil'am]; and-offered-up [va-ya'al] Balak [Balaq] and-Balaam [u-Bil'am] a-bull [par] and-a-ram [va-ayil] on-[each]-altar [ba-mizbeakh].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּעַל (va-ya'al) = "and he offered up" — from עלה (alah) in the hiphil, the technical term for making a burnt offering ascend upon the altar. The singular פָּר וָאַיִל (par va-ayil, "a bull and a ram") with the singular בַּמִּזְבֵּחַ (ba-mizbeakh, "on the altar") indicates that one bull and one ram were placed on each of the seven altars — a distributive singular. Both Balak and Balaam participate in the offering, underscoring their joint cultic action. **LXX:** καὶ ἀνήνεγκεν μόσχον καὶ κριὸν ἐπὶ τὸν βωμόν ("and he offered up a calf and a ram upon the altar").

**3** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] to-Balak [le-Balaq]: "Station-yourself [hityatsev] by [al] your-burnt-offering [olathekha], and-I-shall-go [ve-elekhah]; perhaps [ulai] YHWH will-come-to-meet [yiqqareh] to-me [liqrati], and-the-word [u-dvar] which [mah] he-shall-show-me [yar'eni] I-shall-declare [ve-higgadti] to-you [lakh]." And-he-went [va-yelekh] to-a-barren-height [shefi].

> **Key terms:** הִתְיַצֵּב (hityatsev) = "station yourself" — the hitpael imperative of יצב (yatsav), meaning to take a fixed position and wait. עֹלָתֶךָ (olathekha) = "your burnt offering" — the possessive suffix assigns ownership of the sacrifice to Balak; it is his offering, his expense. יִקָּרֶה (yiqqareh) = "he will come to meet/encounter" — from קרה (qarah), meaning an encounter that may be unexpected or divinely arranged. This is not the usual verb for prophetic revelation but suggests Balaam is uncertain whether God will meet him. שֶׁפִי (shefi) = "a barren height/bare hilltop" — a stripped, windswept elevation suitable for solitary encounter with the divine. The word occurs only here and in Isaiah 41:18 and 49:9 in this sense. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπορεύθη εὐθεῖαν ("and he went straight/directly") — the LXX renders שֶׁפִי as an adverb of manner rather than a noun of place.

**4** And-met [va-yiqqar] God [Elohim] Balaam [Bil'am], and-he-said [va-yomer] to-him [elav]: "[et] the-seven [shiv'at] altars [ha-mizbekhot] I-have-arranged [arakhti], and-I-have-offered-up [va-a'al] a-bull [par] and-a-ram [va-ayil] on-[each]-altar [ba-mizbeakh]."

> **Key terms:** וַיִּקָּר (va-yiqqar) = "and he met/encountered" — from קרה (qarah, "to meet/happen upon"). The narrator uses Elohim rather than YHWH for the divine approach, consistent with the pattern of chapter 22. Balaam immediately reports his ritual preparations as if submitting credentials: עָרַכְתִּי (arakhti) = "I have arranged/set in order" — from ערך (arakh, "to arrange"), the same verb used for arranging sacrificial wood on an altar (Gen 22:9; Lev 1:7). Balaam treats the sacrifices as a means to compel divine attention — a divinatory mindset that YHWH will override. **LXX:** καὶ ἐφάνη ὁ θεὸς τῷ Βαλααμ ("and God appeared to Balaam") — the LXX uses ἐφάνη ("appeared"), elevating the encounter beyond a mere "meeting" to a theophanic event.

**5** And-put [va-yasem] YHWH a-word [davar] in-the-mouth of [be-fi] Balaam [Bil'am], and-said [va-yomer]: "Return [shuv] unto [el] Balak [Balaq], and-thus [ve-khoh] you-shall-speak [tedabber]."

> **Key terms:** וַיָּשֶׂם יְהוָה דָּבָר בְּפִי בִלְעָם (va-yasem YHWH davar be-fi Bil'am) = "and YHWH put a word in the mouth of Balaam" — the subject shifts from Elohim (v. 4) to YHWH, indicating that the word placed in Balaam's mouth comes from Israel's covenant God. The verb שׂים (sim, "to put/place") treats the prophetic word as a concrete object inserted into the mouth — language paralleled in Jeremiah 1:9 ("I have put my words in your mouth") and Deuteronomy 18:18. שׁוּב (shuv) = "return" — the first occurrence of this key verb in the chapter; Balaam is sent back to deliver what YHWH has placed in him. **LXX:** καὶ ἐνέβαλεν ὁ θεὸς ῥῆμα εἰς τὸ στόμα Βαλααμ ("and God cast/threw a word into the mouth of Balaam") — the verb ἐνέβαλεν ("cast into") is more forceful than the Hebrew שׂים, suggesting a more invasive divine action.

**6** And-he-returned [va-yashov] unto-him [elav], and-behold [ve-hinneh] he-was-standing [nitsav] by [al] his-burnt-offering [olato], he [hu] and-all [ve-khol] the-princes of [sarei] Moab [Mo'av].

> **Key terms:** Balaam returns to find Balak exactly where he left him — נִצָּב עַל עֹלָתוֹ (nitsav al olato, "standing by his burnt offering") — obediently stationed beside the sacrifice as Balaam had instructed (v. 3). The detail that all the princes of Moab are present emphasizes the public, state-level character of the event: this is a national act of cursing, witnessed by the ruling elite. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπεστράφη πρὸς αὐτόν, καὶ ὅδε ἐφειστήκει ἐπὶ τῶν ὁλοκαυτωμάτων αὐτοῦ ("and he returned to him, and there he was standing over his whole-burnt-offerings").

**7** And-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "From-Aram [min-Aram] Balak [Balaq] leads-me [yankheni], the-king of [melekh] Moab [Mo'av], from-the-mountains of [me-harerei] the-east [qedem]: 'Come [lekhah], curse [arah] for-me [li] Jacob [Ya'aqov]; and-come [u-lekhah], denounce [zo'amah] Israel [Yisra'el].'

> **Key terms:** וַיִּשָּׂא מְשָׁלוֹ (va-yissa meshalo) = "and he took up his oracle/parable" — the noun מָשָׁל (mashal) means "parable, proverb, oracle, prophetic utterance." The verb נשׂא (nasa, "to lift up") combined with mashal is the standard formula introducing a formal prophetic pronouncement (cf. 23:18; 24:3, 15, 20, 21, 23). מִן אֲרָם (min Aram) = "from Aram" — Aram here refers to the Aramean homeland of upper Mesopotamia, confirming Balaam's origin near the Euphrates (22:5). הַרְרֵי קֶדֶם (harerei qedem) = "the mountains of the east" — the elevated terrain of the eastern Mesopotamian region. זֹעֲמָה (zo'amah) = "denounce" — from זעם (za'am, "to be indignant, denounce"), a strong term for expressing divine fury. Balak's request is stated in poetic parallelism: curse (אָרָה) // denounce (זָעַם), Jacob // Israel. **LXX:** ἐξ Μεσοποταμίας μετεπέμψατό με Βαλακ βασιλεὺς Μωαβ, ἐξ ὀρέων ἀπ' ἀνατολῶν ("from Mesopotamia Balak king of Moab summoned me, from mountains of the east") — rendering אֲרָם as Μεσοποταμία, the standard Greek equivalent.

**8** How [mah] shall-I-curse [eqqov] [that which] God [El] has-not [lo] cursed [qabboh]? And-how [u-mah] shall-I-denounce [ez'om] [that which] YHWH has-not [lo] denounced [za'am]?

> **Key terms:** מָה אֶקֹּב לֹא קַבֹּה אֵל (mah eqqov lo qabboh El) = "how shall I curse what God has not cursed?" — the rhetorical question establishes the theological foundation of the entire Balaam cycle. The verb קבב (qavav, "to curse/execrate") appears in both the finite (אֶקֹּב, eqqov) and the infinitive absolute (קַבֹּה, qabboh) — note the infinitive absolute form used to express the emphasis of God's non-cursing. The divine name אֵל (El, "God") is used in the first colon, YHWH in the second — poetic variation that affirms both the universal God and Israel's covenant God refuse to curse this people. The parallel verbs are curse (קבב) // denounce (זעם), matching Balak's request in v. 7. **LXX:** τί ἀράσωμαι ὃν μὴ ἀρᾶται κύριος; ἢ τί καταράσωμαι ὃν μὴ καταρᾶται ὁ θεός; ("how shall I curse whom the Lord does not curse? Or how shall I execrate whom God does not execrate?") — the LXX reverses the order of the divine names (κύριος first, then ὁ θεός).

**9** For [ki] from-the-top of [me-rosh] rocks [tsurim] I-see-him [er'ennu], and-from-the-hills [u-mi-geva'ot] I-behold-him [ashurennu]. Behold [hen], a-people [am] that-alone [le-vadad] shall-dwell [yishkon], and-among-the-nations [u-va-goyim] it-shall-not [lo] reckon-itself [yitkhashav].

> **Key terms:** מֵרֹאשׁ צֻרִים (me-rosh tsurim) = "from the top of rocks/crags" — Balaam speaks from the high place of Bamoth-Baal (22:41), looking down at Israel. The verb אֲשׁוּרֶנּוּ (ashurennu) = "I behold him" — from שׁור (shur, "to gaze/observe"), a poetic synonym of ראה. לְבָדָד יִשְׁכֹּן (le-vadad yishkon) = "alone it shall dwell" — the adjective בָּדָד (badad, "alone, isolated, solitary") describes Israel's unique standing among the nations. This is not a curse of isolation but a declaration of distinctiveness: Israel exists in a category apart. בַּגּוֹיִם לֹא יִתְחַשָּׁב (va-goyim lo yitkhashav) = "among the nations it shall not reckon itself" — the hitpael of חשׁב (khashav, "to think/reckon") meaning "to count/reckon itself among." Israel cannot be numbered or classified among ordinary nations. **LXX:** ὅτι ἀπὸ κορυφῆς ὀρέων ὄψομαι αὐτὸν καὶ ἀπὸ βουνῶν προσνοήσω αὐτόν. ἰδοὺ λαὸς μόνος κατοικήσει καὶ ἐν ἔθνεσιν οὐ συλλογισθήσεται ("for from the top of mountains I shall see him and from hills I shall observe him. Behold, a people that shall dwell alone and shall not be reckoned among the nations").

**10** Who [mi] has-counted [manah] the-dust of [afar] Jacob [Ya'aqov], and-[who can]-number [u-mispar] the-fourth-part of [rova] Israel [Yisra'el]? Let-die [tamot] my-soul [nafshi] the-death of [mot] the-upright [yesharim], and-let-be [u-tehi] my-end [akhariti] like-his [kamohu]!"

> **Key terms:** מִי מָנָה עֲפַר יַעֲקֹב (mi manah afar Ya'aqov) = "who has counted the dust of Jacob?" — an allusion to the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 13:16, "I will make your seed as the dust of the earth"). The word עָפָר (afar, "dust") evokes innumerability. רֹבַע יִשְׂרָאֵל (rova Yisra'el) = "the fourth part of Israel" — the noun רֹבַע (rova) from the root רבע ("to be square, to divide into four") refers to a quarter of the camp. Even one-fourth of Israel is uncountable. תָּמֹת נַפְשִׁי מוֹת יְשָׁרִים (tamot nafshi mot yesharim) = "let my soul die the death of the upright" — Balaam, overwhelmed by the vision of Israel's blessedness, wishes for a share in their destiny. The plural יְשָׁרִים (yesharim, "upright ones") may contain a wordplay on יִשְׂרָאֵל (Yisra'el) through the root ישׁר (yashar, "upright"). אַחֲרִיתִי (akhariti) = "my end/future" — from אַחֲרִית (akharit), the final outcome or destiny. **LXX:** τίς ἐξηκριβάσατο τὸ σπέρμα Ιακωβ, καὶ τίς ἐξαριθμήσεται δήμους Ισραηλ; ἀποθάνοι ἡ ψυχή μου ἐν ψυχαῖς δικαίων, καὶ γένοιτο τὸ σπέρμα μου ὡς τὸ σπέρμα τούτων ("who has precisely counted the seed of Jacob, and who shall number the people of Israel? May my soul die among the souls of the righteous, and may my seed be as the seed of these") — the LXX reads σπέρμα ("seed") for עָפָר ("dust") and renders רֹבַע as δήμους ("peoples/communities"), departing significantly from the MT.

---

## Balak's First Protest (vv. 11–12)

**11** And-said [va-yomer] Balak [Balaq] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "What [meh] have-you-done [asita] to-me [li]? To-curse [laqov] my-enemies [oyevai] I-took-you [leqakhtikha], and-behold [ve-hinneh], blessing [barekh] you-have-blessed [berakhta]!"

> **Key terms:** לָקֹב אֹיְבַי לְקַחְתִּיךָ (laqov oyevai leqakhtikha) = "to curse my enemies I took you" — the infinitive construct לָקֹב (laqov, from קבב) states the purpose of Balaam's hire. Balak's outrage is expressed through the infinitive absolute construction: בָּרֵךְ בֵּרַכְתָּ (barekh berakhta) = "blessing you have blessed" — the doubling emphasizing the completeness and intensity of the blessing that emerged instead of the expected curse. The irony is that Balak hired Balaam to curse, but the very first utterance is an unreserved blessing. **LXX:** τί πεποίηκάς μοι; εἰς κατάρασιν ἐχθρῶν μου κέκληκά σε, καὶ ἰδοὺ εὐλόγηκας εὐλογίαν ("what have you done to me? To curse my enemies I called you, and behold you have blessed with a blessing").

**12** And-he-answered [va-ya'an] and-said [va-yomer]: "Is-it-not [ha-lo] [et] that-which [asher] YHWH puts [yasim] in-my-mouth [be-fi], [et]-that [oto] I-shall-take-heed [eshmor] to-speak [le-dabber]?"

> **Key terms:** אֵת אֲשֶׁר יָשִׂים יְהוָה בְּפִי אֹתוֹ אֶשְׁמֹר לְדַבֵּר (et asher yasim YHWH be-fi oto eshmor le-dabber) = "that which YHWH puts in my mouth, that I shall guard to speak" — Balaam's defense is straightforward: he is bound to speak only what YHWH places in his mouth. The verb שׁמר (shamar, "to guard/keep/take heed") elevates the obligation beyond mere compliance to watchful, careful stewardship of the divine word. The rhetorical question (הֲלֹא, ha-lo, "is it not?") appeals to what Balak should already know from 22:38. **LXX:** οὐχὶ ὅσα ἐὰν ἐμβάλῃ ὁ θεὸς εἰς τὸ στόμα μου, τοῦτο φυλάξω λαλῆσαι; ("shall I not guard to speak whatever God shall cast into my mouth?").

---

## Relocation and the Second Oracle (vv. 13–26)

**13** And-said [va-yomer] unto-him [elav] Balak [Balaq]: "Come [lekh] please [na] with-me [itti] unto [el] a-place [maqom] another [akher] where [asher] you-shall-see-him [tir'ennu] from-there [mi-sham] — only [efes] the-end of [qatsehu] him you-shall-see [tir'eh] and-all of [ve-khullo] him you-shall-not [lo] see [tir'eh] — and-curse-him [ve-qovenu] for-me [li] from-there [mi-sham]."

> **Key terms:** Balak's strategy shifts from brute force to manipulation of perspective. He will take Balaam to a different vantage point where only קָצֵהוּ (qatsehu, "his end/edge") — the outermost fringe — of Israel's camp is visible. The logic is sympathetic magic: if Balaam sees only a part of Israel, perhaps he can curse that part, or perhaps the reduced visual impression will weaken the sense of Israel's overwhelming multitude. אֶפֶס (efes) = "only/merely" — the limiting particle. The triple repetition of the root ראה (ra'ah, "to see") — תִּרְאֶנּוּ ... תִּרְאֶה ... תִּרְאֶה — keeps the theme of sight at the surface. **LXX:** δεῦρο δὴ μετ᾿ ἐμοῦ εἰς τόπον ἄλλον ἐξ οὗ οὐκ ὄψῃ αὐτὸν ἐκεῖθεν ἀλλ᾿ ἢ μέρος τι αὐτοῦ ὄψῃ, πάντας δὲ οὐ μὴ ἴδῃς ("come with me to another place from which you shall not see him from there, but only a part of him you shall see, and all of them you shall certainly not see").

**14** And-he-took-him [va-yiqqakhehu] to-the-field of [sedeh] Zophim [Tsofim], unto [el] the-top of [rosh] the-Pisgah [ha-Pisgah], and-he-built [va-yiven] seven [shiv'ah] altars [mizbekhot], and-he-offered-up [va-ya'al] a-bull [par] and-a-ram [va-ayil] on-[each]-altar [ba-mizbeakh].

> **Key terms:** שְׂדֵה צֹפִים (sedeh Tsofim) = "the field of the watchers/lookouts" — from צפה (tsafah, "to watch, look out, keep watch"). The name suggests a promontory used for military surveillance, fitting Balak's strategy of finding a better vantage point. רֹאשׁ הַפִּסְגָּה (rosh ha-Pisgah) = "the top of the Pisgah" — the same ridgeline from which Moses will later view the Promised Land (Deut 3:27; 34:1). The Pisgah is part of the Abarim range east of the Dead Sea. The sacrificial protocol is identical to v. 1: seven altars, a bull and ram on each. **LXX:** καὶ παρέλαβεν αὐτὸν εἰς ἀγροῦ σκοπιὰν ἐπὶ κορυφὴν λελαξευμένου ("and he took him to a field of watching, upon the summit of the hewn [height]") — rendering פִּסְגָּה as λελαξευμένου ("hewn/carved"), interpreting the name as deriving from פסג ("to cleave/cut").

**15** And-he-said [va-yomer] unto [el] Balak [Balaq]: "Station-yourself [hityatsev] here [koh] by [al] your-burnt-offering [olathekha], and-I [ve-anokhi] shall-encounter [iqqareh] there [koh]."

> **Key terms:** The same ritual instruction as v. 3: Balak is to stand by his offering while Balaam goes to meet God. The pronoun אָנֹכִי (anokhi, "I") with the verb אִקָּרֶה (iqqareh, "I shall encounter/be met") — the niphal of קרה — implies that Balaam will position himself to be encountered by God, not that he controls the meeting. The adverb כֹּה (koh, "here/there") appears twice with different referents: "here" (where Balak stands) and "there" (where Balaam goes). **LXX:** καὶ ἐπιστηθι ἐπὶ τῆς θυσίας σου, ἐγὼ δὲ πορεύσομαι ἐπερωτῆσαι τὸν θεόν ("and stand upon your sacrifice, and I shall go to inquire of God") — the LXX makes explicit what the MT leaves implied: Balaam goes to inquire (ἐπερωτῆσαι) of God.

**16** And-met [va-yiqqar] YHWH Balaam [Bil'am] and-put [va-yasem] a-word [davar] in-his-mouth [be-fiv] and-said [va-yomer]: "Return [shuv] unto [el] Balak [Balaq], and-thus [ve-khoh] you-shall-speak [tedabber]."

> **Key terms:** The sequence of v. 5 is now compressed: YHWH both meets and implants the word in a single verse. The narrator uses YHWH (not Elohim) for the encounter this time, unlike v. 4 where Elohim met Balaam. This shift may indicate a more direct engagement by Israel's covenant God as the oracles progress. The formulaic instruction — שׁוּב אֶל בָּלָק וְכֹה תְדַבֵּר (shuv el Balaq ve-khoh tedabber) — is identical to v. 5. **LXX:** καὶ συνήντησεν ὁ θεὸς τῷ Βαλααμ καὶ ἐνέβαλεν ῥῆμα εἰς τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ ("and God met Balaam and cast a word into his mouth").

**17** And-he-came [va-yavo] unto-him [elav], and-behold-him [ve-hinno] standing [nitsav] by [al] his-burnt-offering [olato], and-the-princes of [ve-sarei] Moab [Mo'av] with-him [itto]. And-said [va-yomer] to-him [lo] Balak [Balaq]: "What [meh] did-speak [dibber] YHWH?"

> **Key terms:** Balak's question — מַה דִּבֶּר יְהוָה (mah dibber YHWH, "what did YHWH speak?") — is remarkable: Balak, the Moabite king, uses the Tetragrammaton. Whether this reflects his adoption of Balaam's theological language or the narrator's perspective is debated. The scene is set identically to v. 6: Balak standing by his offering, princes attending. The suffix form הִנּוֹ (hinno, "behold him") is the third-person masculine singular of the presentative particle הִנֵּה. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Βαλακ Τί ἐλάλησεν κύριος; ("and Balak said to him, 'What did the Lord speak?'").

**18** And-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "Rise [qum], Balak [Balaq], and-hear [u-shma]; give-ear [ha'azinah] unto-me [adai], son of [ben] Zippor [Tsippor].

> **Key terms:** The second oracle opens with direct address to Balak — an escalation from the first oracle, which referred to Balak in the third person (v. 7). The imperatives קוּם (qum, "rise/stand") and שְׁמָע (shema, "hear") demand attention. הַאֲזִינָה (ha'azinah) = "give ear" — from אזן (azan, "to listen/give ear"), a more solemn and intensive form of listening than שׁמע. עָדַי (adai) = "unto me" — a poetic form of the preposition עַד ("unto/as far as"), with first-person singular suffix. The address "son of Zippor" (בֶּן צִפּוֹר) is patronymic, formal, and authoritative. **LXX:** ἀνάστηθι, Βαλακ, καὶ ἄκουε· ἐνώτισαι, μάρτυς, υἱὸς Σεπφωρ ("rise, Balak, and hear; give ear, O witness, son of Sepphor") — the LXX adds μάρτυς ("witness"), possibly reading עֵדִי for עָדַי.

**19** Not [lo] a-man [ish] [is] God [El] that-he-should-lie [vi-khazzev], and-not [ve-lo] a-son of [ben] man [adam] that-he-should-repent [ve-yitnekham]. Has-he [ha-hu] said [amar] and-shall-not [ve-lo] do [ya'aseh]? And-has-spoken [ve-dibber] and-shall-not [ve-lo] make-it-stand [yeqimenah]?

> **Key terms:** לֹא אִישׁ אֵל וִיכַזֵּב (lo ish El vi-khazzev) = "not a man is God that he should lie" — one of the most theologically consequential statements in the Hebrew Bible. The verb כזב (kazav, "to lie/deceive") in the piel denotes habitual or characteristic lying. וְלֹא בֶן אָדָם וְיִתְנֶחָם (ve-lo ven adam ve-yitnekham) = "and not a son of man that he should repent/change his mind" — the hitpael of נחם (nakham, "to relent/be sorry/change one's mind"). This verse establishes God's immutability of purpose: unlike humans, God does not vacillate. The two rhetorical questions reinforce the point: הַהוּא אָמַר וְלֹא יַעֲשֶׂה — "has he said and will not do?" וְדִבֶּר וְלֹא יְקִימֶנָּה — "and has he spoken and will not make it stand?" The verb קום (qum) in the hiphil (יְקִימֶנָּה, yeqimenah) means "to establish, cause to stand, fulfill." **LXX:** οὐχ ὡς ἄνθρωπος ὁ θεὸς διαρτηθῆναι οὐδὲ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἀπειληθῆναι· αὐτὸς εἴπας, οὐχὶ ποιήσει; λαλήσει, καὶ οὐχὶ ἐμμενεῖ; ("not as a man is God, to be made to waver, nor as a son of man, to be threatened. He having spoken, shall he not do? He shall speak, and shall he not abide?").

**20** Behold [hinneh], to-bless [barekh] I-have-received [laqakhti], and-he-has-blessed [u-verekh] and-I-shall-not [ve-lo] reverse-it [ashivenah].

> **Key terms:** הִנֵּה בָרֵךְ לָקָחְתִּי (hinneh barekh laqakhti) = "behold, to bless I have received" — the infinitive absolute בָרֵךְ (barekh) coupled with the verb לָקַח (laqakh, "to take/receive") means Balaam has received a commission: blessing is his assignment from God. וּבֵרֵךְ וְלֹא אֲשִׁיבֶנָּה (u-verekh ve-lo ashivenah) = "and he has blessed and I shall not reverse it" — the hiphil of שׁוּב (shuv, "to turn back/reverse") with the third-feminine-singular suffix (referring to the blessing). This is the theological climax: the blessing is irreversible. The word שׁוּב, which has structured the chapter (vv. 5, 6, 16), now carries its ultimate meaning — what God has blessed cannot be "turned back." **LXX:** ἰδοὺ εὐλογεῖν παρείλημμαι· εὐλογήσω καὶ οὐ μὴ ἀποστρέψω ("behold, I have been taken to bless; I shall bless and shall certainly not turn back").

**21** He-has-not [lo] beheld [hibbit] iniquity [aven] in-Jacob [be-Ya'aqov], and-he-has-not [ve-lo] seen [ra'ah] trouble [amal] in-Israel [be-Yisra'el]. YHWH his-God [Elohav] [is] with-him [immo], and-the-shout of [teru'at] a-king [melekh] [is] in-him [bo].

> **Key terms:** לֹא הִבִּיט אָוֶן בְּיַעֲקֹב (lo hibbit aven be-Ya'aqov) = "he has not beheld iniquity in Jacob" — the hiphil of נבט (navat, "to look/gaze") indicates an intense, searching gaze: God has looked carefully and found no אָוֶן (aven, "iniquity/wickedness/worthlessness"). The parallel term עָמָל (amal, "trouble/toil/misery") in the second colon broadens the scope: neither moral guilt nor its consequent suffering is found in Israel — from the divine perspective. יְהוָה אֱלֹהָיו עִמּוֹ (YHWH Elohav immo) = "YHWH his God is with him" — the declaration of divine presence. תְּרוּעַת מֶלֶךְ (teru'at melekh) = "the shout of a king" — from רוּעַ (rua, "to shout, raise a war cry"). The "king" is YHWH himself, whose royal battle-shout resounds among his people. **LXX:** οὐκ ἔσται μόχθος ἐν Ιακωβ οὐδὲ ὀφθήσεται πόνος ἐν Ισραηλ· κύριος ὁ θεὸς αὐτοῦ μετ᾿ αὐτοῦ, τὰ ἔνδοξα ἀρχόντων ἐν αὐτῷ ("there shall not be toil in Jacob nor shall pain be seen in Israel; the Lord his God is with him, the glories of rulers are in him") — rendering תְּרוּעַת מֶלֶךְ as τὰ ἔνδοξα ἀρχόντων ("the glories of rulers"), a significant departure from the MT's battle-shout imagery.

**22** God [El] [is] bringing-them-out [motsi'am] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim]; as-the-horns of [ke-to'afot] the-wild-ox [re'em] [are] to-him [lo].

> **Key terms:** אֵל מוֹצִיאָם מִמִּצְרָיִם (El motsi'am mi-Mitsrayim) = "God is bringing them out from Egypt" — the hiphil participle מוֹצִיאָם (motsi'am, "the one bringing them out") presents the exodus as an ongoing divine act, not merely a past event. כְּתוֹעֲפֹת רְאֵם לוֹ (ke-to'afot re'em lo) = "as the horns of a wild ox are to him" — the noun תּוֹעֲפוֹת (to'afot) is a rare term, occurring only here and in 24:8, likely meaning "horns" or "lofty/towering horns" (from יעף, "to be lofty/weary"). The רְאֵם (re'em) is the now-extinct aurochs (Bos primigenius), a massive wild bull — a symbol of untamable power. The imagery declares that God's strength on Israel's behalf is like the horns of this fearsome beast. **LXX:** θεὸς ὁ ἐξαγαγὼν αὐτοὺς ἐξ Αἰγύπτου· ὡς δόξα μονοκέρωτος αὐτῷ ("God who led them out of Egypt; as the glory of a unicorn is to him") — rendering רְאֵם as μονοκέρως ("unicorn/one-horned"), the standard LXX equivalent, based on the ancient understanding of this animal.

**23** For [ki] there-is-no [lo] divination [nakhash] in-Jacob [be-Ya'aqov], and-no [ve-lo] sorcery [qesem] in-Israel [be-Yisra'el]. At-the-time [ka-et] it-shall-be-said [ye'amer] to-Jacob [le-Ya'aqov] and-to-Israel [u-le-Yisra'el]: 'What [mah] has-God [El] done [pa'al]!'

> **Key terms:** לֹא נַחַשׁ בְּיַעֲקֹב וְלֹא קֶסֶם בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל (lo nakhash be-Ya'aqov ve-lo qesem be-Yisra'el) = "there is no divination in Jacob and no sorcery in Israel" — a devastating irony, since Balaam himself is a professional diviner (קֹסֵם, qosem). The noun נַחַשׁ (nakhash, "divination, omen-reading") and קֶסֶם (qesem, "sorcery, lot-casting") refer to the pagan mantic arts. Israel needs no such techniques because God communicates directly: כָּעֵת יֵאָמֵר (ka-et ye'amer) = "at the [proper] time it shall be said" — the niphal imperfect of אמר indicates that at the appropriate moment, God's deeds are simply declared. מַה פָּעַל אֵל (mah pa'al El) = "what has God done!" — an exclamation of wonder at God's mighty acts, which speak for themselves. **LXX:** οὐ γάρ ἐστιν οἰωνισμὸς ἐν Ιακωβ οὐδὲ μαντεία ἐν Ισραηλ· κατὰ καιρὸν ῥηθήσεται Ιακωβ καὶ τῷ Ισραηλ Τί ἐπιτελέσει ὁ θεός ("for there is no augury in Jacob nor divination in Israel; in due time it shall be spoken to Jacob and to Israel: 'What shall God accomplish!'").

**24** Behold [hen], a-people [am] as-a-lioness [ke-lavi] shall-arise [yaqum], and-as-a-lion [ve-kha-ari] he-shall-lift-himself-up [yitnassa]. He-shall-not [lo] lie-down [yishkav] until [ad] he-eats [yokhal] prey [teref], and-the-blood of [ve-dam] the-slain [khalalim] he-shall-drink [yishteh]."

> **Key terms:** כְּלָבִיא יָקוּם (ke-lavi yaqum) = "as a lioness he shall arise" — the לָבִיא (lavi) is specifically the lioness, known for hunting prowess. וְכַאֲרִי יִתְנַשָּׂא (ve-kha-ari yitnassa) = "and as a lion he shall lift himself up" — the אֲרִי (ari) is the mature male lion in his full power. The hitpael of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift up") denotes rising to full stature. The lion imagery declares Israel an unconquerable predator: לֹא יִשְׁכַּב עַד יֹאכַל טֶרֶף (lo yishkav ad yokhal teref) = "he shall not lie down until he eats prey" — this lion does not rest until its hunt is complete. וְדַם חֲלָלִים יִשְׁתֶּה (ve-dam khalalim yishteh) = "and the blood of the slain he shall drink" — חֲלָלִים (khalalim, "slain/pierced ones") from חלל (khalal, "to pierce"). The graphic image of drinking the blood of the slain is a metaphor for total military victory. This verse echoes Jacob's blessing of Judah in Genesis 49:9. **LXX:** ἰδοὺ λαὸς ὡς σκύμνος ἀναστήσεται καὶ ὡς λέων γαυριωθήσεται· οὐ κοιμηθήσεται ἕως φάγῃ θήραν καὶ αἷμα τραυματιῶν πίεται ("behold, a people as a lion's cub shall arise and as a lion shall exult; he shall not sleep until he eats prey and shall drink the blood of the wounded").

---

## Balak's Second Protest (vv. 25–26)

**25** And-said [va-yomer] Balak [Balaq] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "Neither [gam] cursing [qov] you-shall-curse-him [tiqqovenu], nor [gam] blessing [barekh] you-shall-bless-him [tevarakhenu]!"

> **Key terms:** גַּם קֹב לֹא תִקֳּבֶנּוּ גַּם בָּרֵךְ לֹא תְבָרְכֶנּוּ (gam qov lo tiqqovenu gam barekh lo tevarakhenu) = "neither cursing shall you curse him, nor blessing shall you bless him" — Balak's frustration reaches a new level. Both clauses use the infinitive absolute construction (קֹב ... תִקֳּבֶנּוּ and בָּרֵךְ ... תְבָרְכֶנּוּ), the emphatic doubling. Having failed to get a curse, Balak would settle for silence: if you cannot curse, at least do not bless! The repeated גַּם ... גַּם (gam ... gam) = "neither ... nor" construction expresses exasperation. **LXX:** οὔτε κατάραις καταράσῃ μοι αὐτὸν οὔτε εὐλογῶν μὴ εὐλογήσῃς αὐτόν ("neither cursing shall you curse him for me, nor blessing shall you bless him").

**26** And-answered [va-ya'an] Balaam [Bil'am] and-said [va-yomer] unto [el] Balak [Balaq]: "Did-I-not [ha-lo] speak [dibbarti] unto-you [elekha] saying [le'mor]: 'All [kol] that [asher] YHWH shall-speak [yedabber], [et]-that [oto] I-shall-do [e'eseh]'?"

> **Key terms:** Balaam's answer refers back to his declaration in 22:38 — he is bound to speak whatever YHWH commands. The question format (הֲלֹא, ha-lo, "did I not?") is a gentle rebuke: Balak was warned from the beginning. The verb עשׂה (asah, "to do") rather than דבר (dabber, "to speak") is significant: Balaam does not merely relay words — he performs YHWH's will. **LXX:** οὐκ ἐλάλησά σοι λέγων Τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐὰν λαλήσῃ ὁ θεός, τοῦτο ποιήσω; ("did I not speak to you saying, 'The word which God shall speak, this I shall do'?").

---

## Preparation for the Third Attempt (vv. 27–30)

**27** And-said [va-yomer] Balak [Balaq] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "Come [lekhah] please [na], I-shall-take-you [eqqakhakha] unto [el] a-place [maqom] another [akher]; perhaps [ulai] it-shall-be-right [yishar] in-the-eyes of [be-einei] God [ha-Elohim] and-you-shall-curse-him [u-qavotho] for-me [li] from-there [mi-sham]."

> **Key terms:** אוּלַי יִישַׁר בְּעֵינֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (ulai yishar be-einei ha-Elohim) = "perhaps it shall be right in the eyes of God" — the verb ישׁר (yashar, "to be straight/right/pleasing") in the eyes of God suggests Balak now concedes that divine approval is the determining factor. His use of הָאֱלֹהִים (ha-Elohim, "the God") rather than YHWH may indicate his pagan perspective: he addresses the deity generically rather than by covenant name. The repeated pattern — new location, new attempt — demonstrates Balak's persistent but futile hope that geography might overcome theology. **LXX:** δεῦρο παραλάβω σε εἰς τόπον ἄλλον, εἰ ἀρέσει τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἄρασαί μοι αὐτὸν ἐκεῖθεν ("come, I shall take you to another place; if it should please God, then curse him for me from there").

**28** And-took [va-yiqqakh] Balak [Balaq] [et] Balaam [Bil'am] to-the-top of [rosh] the-Peor [ha-Pe'or] the-one-overlooking [ha-nishqaf] upon [al] the-face of [penei] the-wasteland [ha-yeshimon].

> **Key terms:** רֹאשׁ הַפְּעוֹר (rosh ha-Pe'or) = "the top of the Peor" — this is the mountain associated with the deity Baal-Peor (בַּעַל פְּעוֹר), who will feature in the devastating apostasy of chapter 25. The name פְּעוֹר (Pe'or) may derive from פער (pa'ar, "to open wide"), possibly referring to a gaping chasm or the wide-open cultic practices associated with the site. הַנִּשְׁקָף (ha-nishqaf) = "the one overlooking" — the niphal participle of שׁקף (shaqaf, "to look out/overlook"), describing the mountain's commanding view. הַיְשִׁימֹן (ha-yeshimon) = "the wasteland/desert" — the barren stretch of the Jordan Valley visible from the heights of Moab. **LXX:** καὶ παρέλαβεν Βαλακ τὸν Βαλααμ ἐπὶ κορυφὴν τοῦ Φογωρ τὸ παρατεῖνον εἰς τὴν ἔρημον ("and Balak took Balaam to the summit of Phogor, the one extending toward the wilderness").

**29** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] Balak [Balaq]: "Build [beneh] for-me [li] here [ba-zeh] seven [shiv'ah] altars [mizbekhot], and-prepare [ve-hakhen] for-me [li] here [ba-zeh] seven [shiv'ah] bulls [parim] and-seven [ve-shiv'ah] rams [eilim]."

> **Key terms:** The instruction is word-for-word identical to v. 1: שִׁבְעָה מִזְבְּחֹת ... שִׁבְעָה פָרִים וְשִׁבְעָה אֵילִם. The repetition of the identical ritual formula three times (vv. 1, 14, 29) creates the chapter's structural backbone. Despite two failures, the sacrificial protocol does not change — Balaam maintains the same ritual form, underscoring that the outcome depends not on the ritual but on YHWH's sovereign word. **LXX:** identical formula to v. 1.

**30** And-did [va-ya'as] Balak [Balaq] as-which [ka-asher] said [amar] Balaam [Bil'am], and-he-offered-up [va-ya'al] a-bull [par] and-a-ram [va-ayil] on-[each]-altar [ba-mizbeakh].

> **Key terms:** Balak's compliance mirrors v. 2. The chapter ends with the sacrifice prepared but the oracle not yet delivered — the third oracle will open chapter 24, where the narrative takes a dramatic turn as Balaam abandons divination altogether (24:1) and the Spirit of God comes upon him directly. The chapter's final verse thus functions as a cliffhanger: the seven altars are smoking on the peak of Peor, Balak waits, and the reader knows that YHWH's blessing will again prevail. **LXX:** καὶ ἐποίησεν Βαλακ καθάπερ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Βαλααμ, καὶ ἀνήνεγκεν μόσχον καὶ κριὸν ἐπὶ τὸν βωμόν ("and Balak did as Balaam said to him, and offered up a calf and a ram upon the altar").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 176–177)

1. **Chapter location and column layout:** Numbers 23 begins on page 176 of the Leningrad Codex, continuing directly from the end of chapter 22 (which concludes with v. 41 in the third column of the same page). The chapter flows through the remainder of page 176 and continues onto page 177. The standard three-column layout is maintained throughout, with the Masoretic marginal apparatus (Masorah parva) visible between columns and the Masorah magna in the upper and lower margins of both pages.

2. **Parashah divisions within chapter 23:** The Leningrad Codex marks a setumah (ס, closed paragraph) after v. 12 (the conclusion of the first oracle cycle), and another after v. 26 (the conclusion of the second oracle cycle). These divisions correspond precisely to the literary structure of the chapter: first oracle and response (vv. 1–12), second oracle and response (vv. 13–26), and preparation for the third attempt (vv. 27–30). The scribal spacing reinforces the narrative architecture.

3. **The poetic sections (vv. 7–10 and 18–24):** The oracle texts do not display the stichometric (line-by-line) layout sometimes used for poetry in the Leningrad Codex (as seen in Exodus 15 or Judges 5). Instead, the poetry is written in continuous prose format within the column, indistinguishable in layout from the surrounding narrative. Verse divisions are marked only by the standard Masoretic punctuation (sof pasuq and atnakh). This is consistent with the codex's treatment of prophetic oracles embedded within narrative prose.

4. **Scribal precision in divine names:** The alternation of divine names in chapter 23 is carefully preserved in the Leningrad Codex: Elohim in the narrative frame when God approaches (v. 4), YHWH when God implants the word (vv. 5, 16) and in Balaam's oracular speech (vv. 8, 12, 21, 26), and El in the poetic oracles themselves (vv. 8, 19, 22, 23). The scribe makes no errors in this complex pattern, and no marginal corrections or emendations are visible for any divine name in the chapter.

5. **Ketiv/Qere and textual variants:** No ketiv/qere notations are flagged by the Masorah for chapter 23. The text is remarkably stable through this section. The rare term תּוֹעֲפוֹת (to'afot, v. 22) is written without any marginal annotation, suggesting the Masoretes accepted the vocalization as standard despite its rarity (occurring only here and in 24:8).

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pa_0130–0131)

6. **Section markers and chapter navigation:** The Vaticanus manuscript shows section numbering in the margins of pa_0130–0131. The text of Numbers 23 begins in the second column of pa_0130, continuing from chapter 22. The scriptio continua format makes word boundaries invisible, and the oracle sections are not visually distinguished from the narrative prose — consistent with the Vaticanus's general practice throughout the Pentateuch.

7. **LXX divergence at v. 10 — "seed" for "dust":** The most significant textual divergence in the chapter occurs at v. 10, where the Vaticanus reads σπέρμα (sperma, "seed") where the MT has עָפָר (afar, "dust"). This may reflect a different Hebrew Vorlage that read זֶרַע (zera, "seed") instead of עָפָר, or it may be an interpretive rendering linking the oracle to the Abrahamic promises about seed (Gen 15:5) rather than dust (Gen 13:16). The LXX also renders רֹבַע (rova, "fourth part") as δήμους (demous, "peoples/communities"), a substantial departure that eliminates the numerical precision of the MT.

8. **LXX "unicorn" for "wild ox" in v. 22:** The Vaticanus renders רְאֵם (re'em) as μονοκέρως (monokeros, "one-horned animal/unicorn"), the standard LXX equivalent throughout the Old Testament (cf. Ps 22:21; 29:6; 92:10; Job 39:9–10). This translation reflects the ancient Greek understanding of the re'em as a single-horned creature, likely influenced by reports of the Indian rhinoceros or artistic conventions that depicted the aurochs in profile showing only one horn.

9. **LXX addition of "witness" at v. 18:** The Vaticanus appears to read μάρτυς (martys, "witness") in v. 18 where the MT has עָדַי (adai, "unto me"). This may reflect a different Hebrew Vorlage reading עֵדִי (edi, "my witness") or may be an interpretive expansion: the LXX translator understood Balak as being summoned not merely to listen but to serve as a witness to the irreversible divine decree. This reading adds a forensic/legal dimension to the oracle.

10. **LXX rendering of v. 21 — "toil" and "pain" for "iniquity" and "trouble":** The Vaticanus renders אָוֶן (aven, "iniquity") as μόχθος (mokhthos, "toil/hardship") and עָמָל (amal, "trouble") as πόνος (ponos, "pain/grief"). This shifts the meaning from a moral declaration ("God has not seen sin in Israel") to an experiential one ("there shall be no hardship or pain in Israel"). The theological implications are significant: the MT asserts Israel's moral standing before God, while the LXX promises material blessing and freedom from suffering.

### Theological and Structural Notes

11. **The symmetrical architecture of the oracle cycle:** Chapter 23 is built on a rigorously symmetrical pattern, repeated twice with escalation: (a) sacrificial preparation at a high place (vv. 1–2 // vv. 14), (b) Balaam withdraws to seek God (vv. 3 // 15), (c) divine encounter and word-placement (vv. 4–5 // 16), (d) return to Balak (vv. 6 // 17), (e) oracle delivery (vv. 7–10 // 18–24), (f) Balak's protest (vv. 11 // 25), (g) Balaam's defense (vv. 12 // 26). The third cycle begins at vv. 27–30 but breaks the pattern in chapter 24, where Balaam abandons the divinatory process altogether. The progressive breakdown of the ritual framework signals YHWH's increasing directness.

12. **The theology of irreversible blessing (v. 20):** Verse 20 — "behold, to bless I have received, and he has blessed, and I shall not reverse it" — stands as the theological thesis of the entire Balaam cycle. The verb שׁוּב (shuv, "to turn back/reverse") carries the weight: what YHWH blesses cannot be un-blessed, un-spoken, or overturned. This is not a statement about Balaam's willingness but about the nature of divine speech itself. When God speaks blessing, it becomes an ontological reality — it enters the structure of the world and cannot be extracted. This principle underlies the entire scriptural theology of covenant: YHWH's promises are irrevocable (cf. Rom 11:29, drawing on this very tradition).

13. **The first oracle: Israel's solitary destiny (vv. 7–10):** The first oracle establishes Israel's uniqueness through three images: (a) the uncursable people (v. 8) — what God has not cursed, no human can; (b) the solitary nation (v. 9) — Israel dwells alone, outside the categories of ordinary nationhood; (c) the innumerable dust (v. 10) — fulfilling the Abrahamic covenant's promise of uncountable descendants. Balaam's personal wish — "let me die the death of the upright" — reveals that even the pagan diviner recognizes the desirability of Israel's destiny, though he ultimately will not choose it (cf. 31:8).

14. **The second oracle: God's character and Israel's power (vv. 18–24):** The second oracle shifts from Israel's status to God's character. It opens with the declaration of divine immutability (v. 19), establishing that God does not lie, repent, or fail to fulfill his word. It then declares the irreversibility of blessing (v. 20), God's refusal to see guilt in Israel (v. 21), the exodus as ongoing divine act (v. 22), the absence of divination in Israel (v. 23), and Israel's lion-like military power (v. 24). The progression moves from theology proper (who God is) to soteriology (what God does for Israel) to eschatology (what Israel will become). The lion imagery of v. 24 echoes Genesis 49:9 (Judah as a lion) and anticipates the messianic overtones of 24:17 (the star and scepter).

15. **The three high places and the geography of failed cursing:** The three locations — Bamoth-Baal (22:41), the field of Zophim on Pisgah (v. 14), and the top of Peor (v. 28) — trace a geographical arc across the heights of Moab. Each site is a pagan cultic center: Bamoth-Baal ("high places of Baal"), Pisgah (associated with the Moabite sanctuary system), and Peor (the mountain of Baal-Peor). Balak's strategy of changing locations assumes that the deity's power is geographically bound — a common ancient Near Eastern belief. YHWH's consistent blessing from every location demolishes this assumption: the God of Israel is not limited to any place. The tragic irony is that the third site, Peor, will become the location of Israel's own spiritual failure in chapter 25.
